pattern: Sort one array and greedily assign the smallest available element that can beat each opposing element (largest-to-smallest), parking leftovers at positions where no element wins.
hints: Think of it as a matching problem: for each element in the second array, you want the cheapest element from the first array that still beats it.

Process the second array from its largest element downward. For each, take the largest remaining element from the first array; if it wins, place it there; otherwise park the smallest remaining element at that position.

Sort nums1. Sort the indices of nums2 by value descending. Use two pointers lo and hi on the sorted nums1: for each nums2 index (largest first), if sorted_n1[hi] > nums2[idx] assign sorted_n1[hi--] there, otherwise assign sorted_n1[lo++] (a guaranteed loser goes to the hardest slot).

edge_inputs.description: textbook case: every winning assignment is clear
some positions have no winning element; losers fill hardest slots
length 1 where nums1 cannot beat nums2
all equal values — no element can ever win
all nums1 identical but some can beat nums2
nums1 sorted descending input (greedy must not rely on initial order)
nums2 has one massive value that no nums1 element can beat
duplicate values in both arrays create subtle assignment choices

stress.description: large random arrays over a moderate value range to stress the greedy pointer logic
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    nums1 = [rng.randint(0, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(0, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums1, nums2]
stress.note: narrow value range maximises ties and forces the loser-parking logic
